title: School board announces RIFs
description: After a special meeting of the Custer School District (CSD) Board of Education April 7 in Custer, a reduction in force was announced. In total, the reduction in force will include nine classified positions of general education paraprofessionals, library aides and office assistants, three certified positions and three-and-a half custodial/maintenance positions. For the most part, this is being addressed through attrition.

title: Commission hears of dwindling reserves
description: Custer County’s cash reserves are slowly dwindling, and if every department within the county spent the entirety of its budget, the county would be financially strapped. That was the message given by county finance officer Dawn McLaughlin at the April 8 meeting of the Custer County Commission, as McLaughlin walked those in the crowd through the county’s past, present and future budget situation.

title: Turning Point USA Faith hub launching
description: A new Turning Point (TP) USA Faith hub is launching in the Southern Hills, including Hill City, Keystone, Custer County and Fall River County. The project is being undertaken by a group of community leaders and spearheaded by third-generation Custer resident Pat Baumann.

title: Fire burns over 5,000 east county acres
description: A rapidly-spreading fire on the eastern side of Custer County torched 5,408 acres last weekend before forward progress on the fire was stopped. The 79 Fire three miles north of Buffalo Gap was reported around 11:35 a.m. on Saturday, April 11. According to the South Dakota Department of Public Safety, it started just east of Hwy. 79 along East French Creek Road in eastern Custer County. State, federal and local resources along with air support were on initial attack of the fire, which burned primarily grass.

title: City council gets update on West Dam progress
description: The second phase of construction on West Dam is winding down and the third phase is in high gear, it was learned at the April 6 meeting of the Custer City Council. Steve Bareis of RESPEC Engineering, one of the firms helping the city through the project, was present at the meeting via video conferencing and walked the council and those in attendance through a slide show of the work that has been completed and the work that is ongoing. The second phase, which is removal of material from the reservoir and embankment excavation, is expected to be wrapped up by early May.

title: 87 Fire
description: Firefighters responded to the five-acre 87 Fire located approximately four miles south of Hill City off of Hwy. 87 (Needles Highway) April 10. Aircraft, including helicopters, an air attack platform and two single engine air tankers worked the fire along with federal, state and local resources. Forward progress was stopped with dozer lines and hose lay around the fire. Firefighters staffed the fire overnight.

title: Huge change for residential neighborhoods discussed
County suspends search for missing Hill City man
Former lodge facing tax deed action
Environmental, tribal concerns persist as fireworks bring ‘rock concert in phone booth’ to Mount Rushmore
Off to the races for 2026 elections
description: Petitions are in, and the results are out. While several town board and city council seats were open across the county, there will only be an election for the Hermosa and Buffalo Gap town boards. The rest did not receive enough petitions to warrant an election. However, the Custer and Elk Mountain School Districts will see an election. The Custer County Commissioner role has the most candidates, and there will be District 30 races this election as well.

title: County enacts hiring freeze
description: Custer County is staring at a potential budget crisis down the road if it doesn’t act now to cut spending. That is the position of at least two Custer County Commission members who have spent several weeks studying the issue, it was revealed at the March 25 meeting of the commission.

title: Water could be next on tap for city
description: With its renovated/upgraded wastewater system set to go online in a couple of months, the City of Custer will likely now focus—at least sometime in the near future—on an upgrade to the entrance on the opposite side of the water system.

title: Commission OKs abatements
description: A property tax/abatement issue that began last November came to a conclusion at the March 25 meeting of the Custer County Commission, as the commission agreed to a full abatement on a pair of Custer County lots while agreeing to split the difference on a requested abatement on six other lots. All told, the county abated $24,147.71 in property taxes for which it has sent out bills.

title: Legislators proud of property tax relief
Petition results for 2026 election
Towns tests over the limit on forever chemicals
Black Hills Energy seeks rate increase
description: By Jason Ferguson

title: Custer County churches celebrate Holy Week
description: Custer County churches are celebrating Holy Week with a number of special services. This year, Maundy Thursday is April 2, Good Friday is April 3 and Easter Sunday is April 5. Custer Lutheran Fellowship ELCA is having a Maundy Thursday service at 6:30 p.m and a Good Friday Crosswalk at 6:30 p.m. Then, an Easter Sunrise Worship service will take place at 6:30 a.m., followed by a worship service at 9:30 a.m.

title: Ad hoc group says ‘more T-shirts needed’ in Custer
description: By Jason Ferguson

title: Community response to Qury Fire ‘impressive’
description: In the aftermath of the Qury Fire, which began March 12, many are marveling at the community’s response to the incident, from providing meals and lodging for evacuees to volunteering hours of time to help emergency services. “Having people and organizations step up like that, it makes the job very rewarding,” said Custer County Emergency Management director Steve Esser. “I can’t say enough about how great it was to have so many people that were a part of how successful it was.”

title: Sheriff’s office talks patrol changes
description: When the City of Custer opted not to enter into a law enforcement contract with   Custer County for law enforcement services within the city, it not only trimmed the county’s law enforcement budget by over $300,000, but it also changed the way the sheriff’s office patrolled the county, county officials say. The results of that change were discussed at the March 11 meeting of the Custer County Commission.

title: Drainfields a hot topic at county meeting
description: Under the road or over the section line? That was the dilemma facing the Custer County Commission at its March 11 meeting. The issue came when Alex Heim told the commission he had a failing drainfield at his VIP Road home. To install a new drainfield he will either need to put a new wastewater pipe under VIP road—to property he owns on the other side of the road for a spot to build a new drainfield—or cross a section line and run onto his neighbors’ property to build the drainfield.

title: Noe promoted, Ferguson demoted
description: Southern Hills Publishing general manager Jason Ferguson has announced the promotion of Esther Noe to general manager, while subsequently announcing he has demoted himself to “door- opening associate,” which he described as being similar to a Wal-Mart greeter only less cheerful and sans a blue vest. Perhaps a toga, he said.

title: Task order change denied
Rebranding on the way for Hill City
EDC tries to solve affordable housing puzzle
New Underwood wildland fire
description: The New Underwood Volunteer Fire Department responded to a report of a wildland fire in the 22000 block of Sharpe Rd, Unincorporated Pennington County. Upon arrival firefighters observed a wind-driven fire burning in cured vegetation under critical fire weather conditions and immediately struck a second alarm. The fire threatened infrastructure, and caused damage to ranching assets. Firefighters contained the fire to approximately 43 acres. No injuries to firefighters, civilians, livestock or domestic animals were reported.

title: Coyote Flats Fire hits Rockerville
State opens 40 more slots in teacher  apprenticeship program
Qury’s final tally is 9,168 acres
description: Eleven days after it started, the Qury Fire Type 3 Interagency Incident Management Team transitioned management of the fire to a Type 4 operation.

title: Hermosa still rife with controversy
description: Between alleged open meeting violations, long an issue in Hermosa, routinely four-hour-long board meetings, publicly scrutinized financial decisions, the revolving door of board chairmanship and the firing and hiring of legal counsel, the Hermosa Board of Trustees has been fraught with contention and near-upheaval for years, and that has not changed in recent weeks and months.

title: School district talks staff attrition
description: The Custer School District (CSD) is looking at implementing attrition and restructuring methods to improve efficiency and decrease costs in the district. Ultimately, the goal is to balance the district’s annual budget while continuing to provide a great education for students. “Attrition is the key to all the changes that we’re trying to make,” said superintendent of schools Dr. Tim Frederick. “It’s trying to become more efficient and more effective with the resources that we have and that we can continue to keep for sustainability for the district going forward.”

title: Harlow’s allegations discussed at Hill City meeting
description: Several people attended the most recent Hill City School District Board of Education meeting to discuss safety concerns involving Harlow’s School Bus Service and the resignation of several drivers in the previous weeks. During the audience with individuals portion of the meeting, Kelli Werlinger, the new location manager for Harlow’s, addressed the board. “I’m sure you’ve noticed there’s been quite a few changes with services the last few weeks,” she began, going on to discuss the driver walk-out that affected both Hill City and Custer services.

title: Fire opt-out rationale given
description: Custer County volunteer fire departments are on borrowed time—literally and figuratively. That was the message delivered at a special March 9 public meeting held to allow the public to hear what local firefighters say are the reasons for a proposed tax opt-out that will be on the June ballot. If approved, the opt-out will allow the county to opt out of its current fire fund tax levy to raise more funds for county fire departments that are not already within a taxing district.

title: Fanned by wind and dry conditions, Qury Fire consumes over 9,000 acres
description: Fire season has come to Custer County, and it’s only the middle of March. A day that started with a Red Flag warning ended in disaster in Custer County Thursday, March 12, when a pair of fires broke out off Hazelrodt Cutoff two miles southeast of Custer that afternoon. Aided by strong winds and dry conditions the fires merged and has so far chewed up 9,059 acres of Custer County land before being mostly corraled by hundreds of firefighters who converged on Custer from local, state and federal agencies. The fire began just after 1 p.m. last Thursday.

title: School resource officer contract approved
description: The Custer School District (CSD) has arranged a two-year contract with the Custer County Sheriff’s Office for a School Resource Officer (SRO) at $65,000 per year. Further details were discussed at the Board of Education March 9 at the Custer board room. Board vice president Travis Hartshorn, board member Heath Reindl and superintendent of schools Dr. Tim Frederick met with the Custer County Commission to discuss the situation and worked out a plan to keep an SRO in the district.

title: Firework lottery dates released
Free bridge not so free anymore
Bus concerns addressed at school board meeting
Emergency closure order issued for Qury Fire
Qury fire now over 7,200 acres
description: The Qury Fire, which is now listed at 7,200a cres, has claimed structures on 10 properties, it was reported last evening during a special community forum regarding the fire held at the Custer Jr./Sr. High School Theater. Custer County Sheriff's Office Lt. Derrick Reifenrath told the assembled crowd that structures lost varied from outbuildings and garages to homes. He did not say how many homes were lost.

title: Ongoing tap talk transpires in Keystone
French Creek lawsuit dismissed
description: A federal judge has dismissed a federal lawsuit aimed at preventing the City of Custer from discharging effluent into French Creek, seemingly clearing the path for the City of Custer to begin the discharge nearly a year after reconstruction and renovation work was complete on its wastewater treatment facility.

title: You can be a child’s new TeamMate
description: By dedicating 30 minutes a week, you could change the life of a student in the Custer School District (CSD) through the TeamMates Mentoring Program. The mission of TeamMates is “To positively impact the world by inspiring students to reach their full potential through mentoring.” TeamMates is a mentorship program designed to occur during a school day. Meetings are held at the school, usually during lunch, so the mentee’s academic schedule is not interrupted. In 2018, CSD was the first school in South Dakota to implement the TeamMates program.

title: County sales tax heads to Rhoden’s desk
description: Two bills that would allow local governments to impose new sales taxes for specific purposes cleared their last legislative hurdles on Thursday at the South Dakota Capitol in Pierre and are headed to the governor for his consideration.

title: Food truck presence questioned
description: The presence of a food truck in Custer—even if for only one day—during Custer Restaurant Week had some restaurant owners fit to be tied. At its March 2 meeting the Custer City Council hard from Custer Area Chamber of Commerce director Dawn Murray came before the council to say she had been contacted by restaurant owners to voice displeasure over the presence of the food truck, which spent a day at Custer Ace Hardware during Restaurant Week. Murray asked how the situation could be rectified for the future.

title: Trading property tax for sales tax
USDA approves emergency forest restoration assistance
description: The U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) Farm Service Agency (FSA) in Custer/Fall River Counties is accepting applications for the Emergency Forest Restoration Program (EFRP) to address damages from the severe windstorm that occurred Dec. 18. EFRP provides financial cost-share and technical assistance to restore nonindustrial private forestland (NIPF) damaged by a qualifying natural disaster. EFRP signup began Jan. 26 and ends March 27.

title: Family resource fair Saturday
description: This Saturday, March 14, the Custer Elementary School gym will transform into a “one-stop shop” for local parents and caregivers. Early Learner Custer invites the community to the third Annual Southern Hills Family Resource Fair, running from 10 a.m. to 1 p.m. This free community event is specifically designed for families with children ages 0–8. It aims to bridge the gap between Southern Hills families and the essential services that help children thrive, from prenatal development through the early elementary years.

title: Carnival coming to CHS prom April 25
description: It’s going to be a night of fun, dancing and celebrating at Custer High School prom Saturday, April 25. This year’s theme is “Rio Carnival,” and attendees can expect an abundance of vibrant, leafy, colorful decorations, including some feathers and balloons. Students should plan to arrive at school around 6 p.m. to pay for their tickets, take some pictures with friends and family members and prepare to line up for the grand march. Tickets are $15 each.

title: Fire opt-out on June 2 ballot
description: Voters in a large swath of Custer County will head to the polls June 2 to decide if they want to supply more money to local volunteer fire departments that are not already part of a taxing district. The vote will affect voters in every corner of the county, save for those located within the boundaries of the Battle Creek Fire District and the Fairburn Fire District, both of which already have formed their own taxing jurisdictions to collect their own revenue  apart from the county levy.

title: New ELA curriculum recommended for school
description: Members of the Custer School District literacy/curriculum development team, including Jacqueline Hagen, Conni Virtue, Hallie Webster and Jenn Doyle, presented their process and recommendation for the next English Language Arts (ELA) curriculum at the board of education meeting April 13 in Hermosa. The literacy/curriculum development team consists of teachers and administrators from Custer and Hermosa. Doyle said it started by partnering with Compass, but realized it could achieve the same thing independently more efficiently and effectively while saving the district money.

title: Town attorney, engineer in crosshairs
description: There was a time last year when citizens were attending Hermosa Board of Trustee meetings with their own popcorn, and after the four-hour April 7 regular meeting, some wondered if that should be revisited. When the agenda for this meeting was published the week prior, there was an item for approving a budget to hire a new town attorney.

title: Lessons learned from Qury Fire
description: Custer County is full of well-trained first responders who are ready to answer the call during an emergency at a moment’s notice. That doesn’t mean there aren’t lessons to be learned in the aftermath of an emergency. Such is the case now over a month removed from the Qury Fire, the fast-moving blaze that burned over 9,000 acres of land a little over two miles southeast of the City of Custer.

title: French Creek flood mitigation site up
description: In 2019, severe rainfall led to flash flooding along French Creek, sending water into streets, homes and businesses through the City of Custer. The event caused widespread disruptions and highlighted how quickly conditions along the creek can become hazardous.

title: Aldermen table proposal, discuss projects
Keystone asking for compound meter
Business manager Cassandra Ott recognized
Body of missing man found
Take it for ‘grant’ed
South Dakota Wildland Fire unveils new mascot
Prevailer to host Hill City candidate forum May 12
Early voting begins in some South Dakota counties after delay
Voters to determine east Custer ambulance
description: Voters on the eastern side of Custer County will head to the polls June 2 to decide whether or not to pay extra taxes to ensure ambulance coverage continues on that side of the county. For the past several months the Custer County Commission has been working with Pennington County, the Rapid City Fire Department and representatives of the eastern side of the county to bring about the vote, if which approved will bring about the creation of the East Custer Ambulance District.

title: Dakota Cowboy Inn given new life as apartments
description: Many times when someone purchases a building, they give it a facelift. As for the Ponderosa Apartments? It received a facelift, triple bypass, appendectomy, heart transplant and facial reconstruction. Yes, the property formerly known as the Dakota Cowboy Inn needed a little more than a fresh coat of paint. Luckily, Tom Iverson and Daniela Malcoci of Link Investment Group were up to the challenge.

title: City aid sought by HFH
description: Black Hills Area Habitat for Humanity wants to build some affordable workforce housing on north 7th Street in Custer. But it will require some help. That was the message delivered to the Custer City Council at the council’s April 20 meeting. Gene Fennell, a volunteer representative for Black Hills Area Habitat for Humanity, along with organization members Mike Keegan and Scott Engmann came before the council to discuss the issue, asking for the city’s help in solving a utility issue that exists on lots that could be used for Habitat housing.

title: Resident upset with social media posts
description: A Custer County Commission member’s recent conduct on social media was unprofessional, according to a Custer County resident who spoke during the public comment portion of the April 22 meeting of the commission. Becky Treloar said after someone posted information about a Custer County Commission candidate on a public Custer community page, attempts to correct misinformation on the post was followed by what she said was unprofessional conduct by commissioner Mike Busskohl.
